question.text: The Sharpe Ratio is calculated as __________, where Rs is the scheme return and Rf is the risk-free return.

answer.text: (Rs - Rf) / Standard Deviation of scheme returns
(Rs - Rf) / Tracking Error of the scheme
Rs / Standard Deviation of scheme returns

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** The Sharpe Ratio measures excess return per unit of total risk (standard deviation). The formula is (Rs - Rf) / Standard Deviation. A higher Sharpe Ratio indicates better risk-adjusted performance. It uses standard deviation as the risk measure because it captures total risk (both systematic and unsystematic).
**The Trap:** (Rs - Rf) / Beta is the Treynor Ratio, not the Sharpe Ratio. Beta measures only systematic risk. Forgetting to subtract the risk-free rate (Rs / Std Dev) ignores the concept of excess return, which is fundamental to risk-adjusted measurement.
**Cross-Exam Context:** Sharpe Ratio is tested across NISM Series V-A and V-B. Understanding the distinction between Sharpe (uses std dev) and Treynor (uses beta) is critical.
**Key Takeaway:** Sharpe uses Standard Deviation (total risk); Treynor uses Beta (systematic risk). Both measure excess return per unit of their respective risk measures.
